Okay, so I want to talk about the four levels of delegation. So what I want to start with is understanding what the goal of, right, okay, so the goal of a leader is to spend your time doing what only you can do, okay, and this is a question that I really wish I could remember where I read it and it was about a year and a half ago, it was when we really started scaling the team very quickly and every morning I'd wake up and I would ask myself, I would look at my to-do list and I would say, what is it that only I can do on this list? And now I do it unintentionally and just kind of habitually how I think, but I really suggest that you start with this in mind, which is your goal should always be to only do the things that only you can do, okay, and so how do you develop leaders? You delegate, okay, so the only way for you to only do what you should be doing and the only way to develop leaders on the team, okay, the issue is that it's not intuitive for most of us to delegate past anything other than our weaknesses, right, because you already know how to do it, you already know the what, the when, the where, and how to do it, you can do it literally 10 times faster than the person, right, and so that's why most people don't delegate anything that they're good at or okay at or even great at, even when it comes time to do that for your team because people don't want to take the time to invest in the person to teach them how to do it to the level that they can, right. Also successful as leaders hinder them from growing others, okay, so to become a successful leader you often have to have a lot of the traits that then start to backfire once you start having to offload things to your team, right, like one is being competitive, right, because to start a business you need to be competitive to some level, okay, two is dismissive of those who don't meet standards, right, three being extremely hard on yourself and then four is a perfectionist mentality, okay, and the reason that this is all going to work against you is that in delegating there will be issues in the beginning, the beginning is the hardest part, they're not going to do it how you need it the first time, they're probably going to mess up, they're probably going to lose you money, very, very like hard point to hit on, but it's very true, like delegating will lose you money, but it will, it is a short-term cost that will gain you long-term stability in the business, because if you're the only person that can do things to the best of your abilities, then there's no way that when you are ever going to actually get out of the business, okay, the business in which you end up being the genius with a thousand helpers, this is a concept that Jim Collins talks about, right, it's basically you start off and it's just you and one person running your gym and then basically before you can grow into a basically meaning like you throw the company on your back and they just become extensions of you, they don't operate on their own, they don't make decisions on their own, like you're just micromanaging your entire team, okay, and it's a very entrepreneurial way of running a business and it's one thing that I really do encourage that you do not do, you as a small business, the biggest strategic demand you have is having an amazing culture and you cannot have an amazing culture if you do not delegate and trust people, okay, so I want to talk about the levels of delegation because when you are delegating to somebody, it really goes through a process and a lot of people say like well I don't know where to start, I don't know what I should give to the person to start off with and so I'm going to break down each level so you understand what you need to put people through in order to get to the level where it's completely delegated to somebody, okay, level one is investigation, all right, so what is that? That is delegation of retrieving information, stats, research, and summarization, right, and so that means say you want to explore a new software or explore a new method for, you know, how you track something, you say hey Susie, I would like you to go research this company for me, get all the stats, talk to their salesperson, do the live demo, and then I would like you to back to me on Tuesday, sound good? Cool, and then what happens is that you take it from there to complete the project, right, so this is maybe it's someone's first or second week and it's the first assignment you're giving them, retriever of information, and then I want them to, level two is informed progress, all right, so what is that? That means that a task is given with the understanding that as long as updates are regularly given to the leader, they can proceed, and so what that means is you would probably do something like meeting once or twice a week to touch base on that task because you want to make sure that they are doing the task, so you don't just, you don't tell the mentor how to do the task, you just tell them what needs to be done, and then you check in with them to make sure the things that need to be done are getting done, right, always making sure that you voice to them that you were there to help, so it's like hey, this is the first project I would like you to take on your own, here's what I need as the outcome of this project, here are the five outcomes I want from this, self, but I will be here touching base with you every Friday to make sure that it's on track, and I also want you to know that if you ever need to reach out to me or you're stuck on something or you need my input on it, that I'm here to help you, and so you can box me or you can email me, and then we can kind of work through it from there, okay, that's the second, so what that means is you give a task to your mentor, and then you don't ask to be updated, you literally just want the results delivered to you once it's complete, okay, and there's actually no interaction until it is complete, so this would be giving someone a project saying I don't want updates on it, nothing, just like once it's delivered by the end of the month, like show me the results, that's all I want to, okay, and that is typically something that I would suggest you give someone after probably around 30 to 60 days, so between probably in their second month being on board with you, you start giving them projects and then showing them the result that you need, and just asking for it once it's done on your own, I trust you to use your resources, I trust that you can make the right decisions to get this outcome, and it's almost a test for them because if you're telling them like don't check in with me, like I want you to do this on your own, I trust that you can figure it out yourself, that really empowers people because a lot of the times people are unsure of what decision to make, and so they always will come to decisions and just get the outcome, and I don't want to be involved in the decision-making process, it really shows people what they're actually trusting them with this, for the most part four out of five times, they're going to make the right decision, and then lastly is level four, which is ownership, okay, and so what that means is a project or task is assigned with the expectation the leader will not be kept in the loop or even made aware of the results to follow, so that means that you trust someone so explicitly that you don't need to hear about the results, you don't want to be updated, literally you just want to say this needs to get done, you can do it, I never want to hear about it again, okay, this is a level that will take time to get to for one person alone over a year in a role to get to a point where you don't need to check back in because it's really completely trust someone with your life, meaning all the other projects that you've done, you've had them delegated to with levels two and little mistakes, you feel like every time they're on top of things, and you trust that they can hold themselves accountable to the result rather than needing you to be pestering them, right, and so that's where the employees at a point where they feel more accountability toward the business and the customers than you, and so that's when people will move things forward, you need to get things done, but they feel accountability to their role, to being excellent, to the customer, to the business to get something done, and so remember when you bring someone on new moving roles, you need to be mindful of your way working through these levels, okay, and so a lot of the times I like to integrate this into the 30-day, 60-day, 90-day onboarding goals that are in above in this training portal, and basically thinking to yourself, okay, in the first 30 days, what can I delegate levels one and two? In the first 30 to 60 days, I would like to focus on level two and three, and then after their 90 days is complete, you know, is there anything that I could delegate to them completely at this point, and so I think it's very important that you be open and transparent about those things and really keep them in mind for a while.
